title: Needle-felted peacock
subtitle: A standing peacock in iridescent blue-green merino with a spread felt tail fan
excerpt: Build a standing peacock in teal and cobalt merino with a large spread tail fan cut from felted fabric and decorated with needle-felted eye markings. The finished peacock stands about 15 cm tall with the tail spread to 20 cm.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Display-bird forms with fabric tail constructions are a standard intermediate British needle-felting project.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. Each stab locks fibres into a progressively firmer mass.
Lightly felted wool sheet that holds together but remains pliable. Used here to cut the peacock tail fan from a flat sheet.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. The standard input material for needle felting.

content.text: has two distinct elements: the body, built from
in the round, and the tail fan cut from
sheet and decorated with needle-felted eye spots. The two elements are made separately and joined at the rump.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a finger guard on the supporting hand at all times.

items.name: Green-gold prefelt sheet for tail fan
Small amounts of bronze, blue, and dark green merino (eye spots)
Craft wire (1.5 mm) for leg armature

content.text: Needle felt a compact egg-shaped body about 8 cm tall from teal merino, with a short thick neck. Add cobalt highlights along the breast and neck with surface needling. Build a simple two-wire leg armature as for the pony entry and work the legs into the underside of the body. The legs support the standing pose; without them the piece will not stand.
Step 2: Make the head and crest

Needle felt a small rounded head in teal, about 2.5 cm across, and join it to the top of the neck. Pull four or five wisps of roving for the crest feathers and needle each one at the root only, leaving the tips free and radiating upward from the top of the head.

Draw a semicircular fan shape on the prefelt, about 20 cm across, with a straight base. Cut it out. Using small amounts of bronze and blue merino, needle felt roughly circular eye spots at irregular intervals across the fan, each about 2 cm across. Work each spot in three layers: a dark centre, a lighter ring, a dark outer edge. Needle each layer in place before adding the next.

Lay the straight base of the fan against the rump of the body. Needle felt along the straight edge, working through the fan base and into the body, to join the two pieces. The fan should stand proud of the body rather than drooping forward. Prop the piece upright to check the tail stays spread; if it droops, stitch the base more firmly or stiffen the base edge with a few strands of wire under the felt.

items.problem: The tail fan droops forward over the body
items.cause: The fan is too heavy relative to the body, or the join is not rigid enough
items.fix: Stiffen the base of the fan by threading a short length of wire along the bottom edge and needling over it. Then reattach to the body more securely.

items.problem: The eye spots look like blobs of colour rather than concentric rings
items.cause: Each colour layer was applied on top of the previous without needling in between
items.fix: Needle each colour layer fully before adding the next. Work from the centre outward, and use a finer needle for the smallest central dot.
